title: Threat Hunting Indicators
dataSources: EDR process telemetry, file events, network connections
spl:
index=edr sourcetype="crowdstrike:events"
| search (process_name="powershell.exe" AND command_line="*-enc*")
    OR (process_name="rundll32.exe" AND parent_process_name!="explorer.exe")
    OR (process_name="certutil.exe" AND command_line="*-urlcache*")
| table _time, hostname, user, process_name, command_line, parent_process_name
description: Proactive threat hunting using EDR telemetry detects stealthy threats that evade automated detection.
value: Proactive threat hunting using EDR telemetry detects stealthy threats that evade automated detection.
implementation: Ingest EDR telemetry (process creation, network connections, file writes). Create hunting queries for LOLBin usage, encoded PowerShell, suspicious parent-child process relationships. Schedule as recurring searches for continuous hunting.
visualization: Table (suspicious indicators), Timeline (hunting hits), Bar chart (indicators by technique).
cimSpl:
| tstats `summariesonly` count
  from datamodel=Endpoint.Processes
  by Processes.process_name Processes.parent_process_name Processes.dest span=1h
| where count>0
| sort -count
dataModelAcceleration: Enable acceleration on the Endpoint data model, dataset Processes, before using this tstats search.
knownFalsePositives: LOLBin use from real installers and Office repair flows; PowerShell from helpdesk, packaging tools, and legitimate remote admin—confirm intent before a major incident is declared.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: EDR TA (telemetry data).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: EDR process telemetry, file events, network connections.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Ingest EDR telemetry (process creation, network connections, file writes). Create hunting queries for LOLBin usage, encoded PowerShell, suspicious parent-child process relationships. Schedule as recurring searches for continuous hunting.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=edr sourcetype="crowdstrike:events"
| search (process_name="powershell.exe" AND command_line="*-enc*")
    OR (process_name="rundll32.exe" AND parent_process_name!="explorer.exe")
    OR (process_name="certutil.exe" AND command_line="*-urlcache*")
| table _time, hostname, user, process_name, command_line, parent_process_name
```

Understanding this SPL

**Threat Hunting Indicators** — Proactive threat hunting using EDR telemetry detects stealthy threats that evade automated detection.

Documented **Data sources**: EDR process telemetry, file events, network connections. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): EDR TA (telemetry data). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: edr; **sourcetype**: crowdstrike:events. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=edr, sourcetype="crowdstrike:events". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Applies an explicit `search` filter to narrow the current result set.
• Pipeline stage (see **Threat Hunting Indicators**): table _time, hostname, user, process_name, command_line, parent_process_name

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats `summariesonly` count
  from datamodel=Endpoint.Processes
  by Processes.process_name Processes.parent_process_name Processes.dest span=1h
| where count>0
| sort -count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**Threat Hunting Indicators** — Proactive threat hunting using EDR telemetry detects stealthy threats that evade automated detection.

Documented **Data sources**: EDR process telemetry, file events, network connections. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): EDR TA (telemetry data). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Endpoint.Processes` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable acceleration on the Endpoint data model, dataset Processes, before using this tstats search.


Step 3 — Validate
Compare a sample in the EDR console (CrowdStrike Falcon, Microsoft Defender for Endpoint portal, Carbon Black, or SentinelOne), the Sysmon Operational log in Event Viewer, or `Get-MpThreatDetection` on Windows, as applicable.

Validation (cross-check)

Compare the same time range in your EDR console and confirm host, user, and technique line up. For Sysmon, open the same Event ID in Sysmon’s Operational log. For Microsoft Defender, run `Get-MpThreatDetection` in elevated PowerShell and match threat and time.


Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (suspicious indicators), Timeline (hunting hits), Bar chart (indicators by technique).
grandmaExplanation: We search our endpoint activity for odd process trees and command lines, because that is how attackers often abuse the same trusted tools you already have installed.
